content.text: When an agent calls a tool, the result feeds directly back into the model's context as a

message. How that result is formatted — including whether it signals success or failure, includes a retry hint, or truncates a large response — determines whether the model can adapt intelligently or gets stuck in a loop.

attrs.title: Three branches: success, schema error, exception

content.text: The feedback message is the model's only window into what happened.
A vague error string gives the model nothing to act on; a typed result with a specific retry hint tells it exactly what to try next.
Wrap every tool execution in a
dataclass before converting it to the API's
dict. This separates tool execution from feedback formatting and makes each failure class testable in isolation.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any
from enum import Enum

class ToolStatus(Enum):
    SUCCESS        = 'success'
    SCHEMA_ERROR   = 'schema_error'    # output exists but wrong shape
    RUNTIME_ERROR  = 'runtime_error'   # tool raised an exception
    TIMEOUT        = 'timeout'         # tool took too long
    EMPTY          = 'empty'           # no output returned

@dataclass
class ToolResult:
    tool_use_id: str
    tool_name: str
    status: ToolStatus
    raw: Any              # exact value returned / exception raised
    feedback: str         # plain-text message for the model
    retry_hint: str = ''  # extra guidance to include on retry

def make_feedback(result: ToolResult) -> dict:
    """Convert ToolResult to the tool_result message dict Claude expects."""
    content = result.feedback
    if result.retry_hint:
        content += f'\n\nHint: {result.retry_hint}'
    return {
        'type': 'tool_result',
        'tool_use_id': result.tool_use_id,
        'content': content,
        'is_error': result.status != ToolStatus.SUCCESS,
    }
field in the API dict is critical: it signals to Claude that the result is not a valid output and that it should change its approach rather than reason from the error string.
function handles every failure mode: unknown tool, timeout, runtime exception, empty result, and schema mismatch. It always returns a
helper checks required keys and types. For tools that return structured dicts, pass an
to catch shape mismatches before they confuse the planner.
The outer loop passes each tool result back to the model. When a result has
, the model will typically try a corrected approach on its next turn. A per-tool retry counter prevents any single broken tool from consuming the entire step budget.

# Pitfall 1: returning a bare string error without is_error=True
# The model sees it as a success and reasons from garbage data.
# BAD:
tool_result_bad = {
    'type': 'tool_result',
    'tool_use_id': block.id,
    'content': 'Error: file not found',  # looks like a result!
    # is_error omitted — defaults to False
}

# GOOD:
tool_result_good = {
    'type': 'tool_result',
    'tool_use_id': block.id,
    'content': 'Error: file not found. Try a different path.',
    'is_error': True,
}

# Pitfall 2: re-inserting a stale assistant message without its tool_results
# Claude's API requires that every tool_use block in an assistant message
# is followed by a matching tool_result in the next user message.
# Sending an assistant turn with tool_use and then a different user turn
# (without tool_result) will cause an API validation error.
the model treats an error string as a valid result and builds incorrect reasoning from it.
every tool_use in an assistant message must have a matching tool_result in the next user message. The API rejects conversations where this contract is broken.
"Something went wrong" gives the model nothing to act on. Include the exception message, the tool name, and a specific retry hint.
, a broken tool can consume all remaining steps while a healthy alternative exists.
Code execution tools produce stdout, stderr, and an exit code. Capture all three and map them to
before returning. Truncate long stdout to the last N lines — the end of output is usually more informative than the middle.
When code exits with a non-zero return code, include the stderr in the feedback — without it the model cannot see the Python traceback that explains the failure.
Write tools (file creation, database inserts, API POSTs) must not run twice with the same input when the agent retries after a timeout. An
and returns a cached success if the call was already confirmed.
the idempotency guard is per-run, not global. It prevents
duplicates (caused by retry loops). Cross-run deduplication requires a persistent store.
Wrap every tool result in a typed
before converting to the API dict — this separates execution from feedback formatting.
for non-success outcomes; without it the model reasons from the error string as if it were valid data.
specific to the failure class: schema errors get the expected schema; runtime errors get "do not retry same call".
Track per-tool retry counts and disable consistently broken tools before they exhaust the step budget.
